records.Name: 42 U.S.C. 4332 — NEPA Action-Forcing Provisions
records.Subject: 42 U.S.C. 4332
records.nepa_compliance_requirements__c: Federal agencies must prepare a detailed environmental impact statement for every major federal action significantly affecting the quality of the human environment. Agencies must assess CE applicability, prepare EAs when significance is uncertain, and prepare a full EIS for significant impacts. NEPA review must be integrated early in planning before irreversible resource commitments. Note: CEQ implementing regulations (40 CFR Parts 1500-1508) were rescinded in 2025; agency-specific NEPA procedures remain operative.
records.nepa_text_content__c: The Congress authorizes and directs that, to the fullest extent possible: (1) the policies, regulations, and public laws of the United States shall be interpreted and administered in accordance with the policies set forth in this chapter, and (2) all agencies of the Federal Government shall... include in every recommendation or report on proposals for legislation and other major Federal actions significantly affecting the quality of the human environment, a detailed statement by the responsible official on — (i) the environmental impact of the proposed action, (ii) any adverse environmental effects which cannot be avoided should the proposal be implemented, (iii) alternatives to the proposed action... — 42 U.S.C. § 4332(2)(C). Categorical exclusions apply where a class of action has been determined not to have significant effect and no extraordinary circumstances are present.

records.Name: 33 U.S.C. 1344 — CWA Section 404 Dredge and Fill Permits
records.Subject: 33 U.S.C. 1344; 33 CFR Part 323
records.nepa_compliance_requirements__c: A Corps of Engineers permit is required before discharging dredged or fill material into waters of the United States, including wetlands. Individual permits required for major impacts; nationwide permits available for minor impacts (e.g., NWP 14 for linear transportation under 0.1 acre wetland loss). Exemptions exist for normal farming, silviculture, and ranching; exemptions void if activity discharges toxic pollutants. The permit program is jointly administered with EPA under Section 404(c) veto authority. NEPA review integrated into the 404 permit evaluation through the USACE public interest review.
records.nepa_text_content__c: 33 CFR Part 323 — Permits for Discharges of Dredged or Fill Material into Waters of the United States. Authority: 33 U.S.C. 1344. Trigger: Any discharge of dredged or fill material (rock, sand, soil, construction debris, material replacing a water body) into waters of the US. Nationwide Permits available for 58 categories of minor activities. Individual Standard Permits required for activities not qualifying for an NWP or Regional GP. Key threshold: NWP 14 covers up to 1/10 acre (0.1 acre) of wetland loss for linear transportation projects. NWP 12 covers utility line crossings. Section 404(b)(1) guidelines (40 CFR Part 230) prohibit permits where practicable alternatives exist with less adverse effect. EPA may veto issuance under Section 404(c). State 401 Water Quality Certification required before Corps can issue permit.

records.Name: 33 U.S.C. 1341 — CWA Section 401 Water Quality Certification
records.Subject: 33 U.S.C. 1341; 40 CFR Part 121
records.nepa_compliance_requirements__c: Any applicant for a federal license or permit to conduct an activity that may result in a discharge into navigable waters must obtain a state (or interstate agency) water quality certification that the discharge will comply with applicable water quality standards. The certifying state has one year to act; failure to act within one year constitutes a waiver. If the state denies certification, the federal permit or license cannot be issued. For CWA 404 permits, 401 certification is a prerequisite to USACE issuance.
records.nepa_text_content__c: 40 CFR Part 121 — Water Quality Certification. Authority: 33 U.S.C. 1341. Any applicant for a Federal license or permit to conduct any activity that may result in any discharge into the navigable waters shall provide the licensing or permitting agency a certification from the State in which the discharge originates that any such discharge will comply with applicable provisions of §§ 301, 302, 303, 306, and 307 of the CWA. State has one year from receipt of complete application to act; deemed waived if no response. State may condition certification. FERC license applicants, NRC licensees, and USACE 404 applicants are among the most common triggers.

records.Name: 33 U.S.C. 1342 — CWA Section 402 National Pollutant Discharge Elimination System
records.Subject: 33 U.S.C. 1342; 40 CFR Part 122
records.nepa_compliance_requirements__c: An NPDES permit is required before discharging any pollutant from a point source into waters of the United States. Construction activity disturbing one or more acres requires an NPDES Construction General Permit (CGP) with a Stormwater Pollution Prevention Plan (SWPPP). Industrial stormwater discharges, municipal separate storm sewer systems (MS4s), and concentrated animal feeding operations (CAFOs) are separately regulated. EPA administers NPDES; 46 states have EPA-approved state programs administering the permit in lieu of EPA.
records.nepa_text_content__c: 40 CFR Part 122 — EPA Administered Permit Programs (NPDES). Authority: 33 U.S.C. 1342. Point source: any discernible, confined, and discrete conveyance (pipe, ditch, channel, tunnel, conduit, well, discrete fissure, container, rolling stock, concentrated animal feeding operation, or vessel). Construction General Permit (CGP) applies to construction activity disturbing >= 1 acre total land surface including smaller common plan of development. SWPPP required before discharge begins. Effluent limitations based on technology-based standards (BAT/BCT) and water quality-based standards. Municipal permits (MS4s) require implementation of best management practices for stormwater runoff.

records.Name: 16 U.S.C. 1536 — ESA Section 7 Interagency Cooperation
records.Subject: 16 U.S.C. 1536; 50 CFR Part 402
records.nepa_compliance_requirements__c: All federal agencies must consult with FWS or NOAA Fisheries before authorizing, funding, or carrying out any action that may affect a listed threatened or endangered species or designated critical habitat. Informal consultation concludes with a written concurrence that the action is 'not likely to adversely affect' listed species. Formal consultation required when action 'may affect' species adversely, concluding with a Biological Opinion. A jeopardy finding or adverse modification finding requires reasonable and prudent alternatives. Formal consultation: 90-day deadline extendable 60 days with applicant consent. Incidental take statement included in no-jeopardy BO authorizes take up to specified levels. Note: statute multiplier 1.48x in NEPA litigation risk model (pending TAILS/PCTS decomposition).
records.nepa_text_content__c: 50 CFR Part 402 — Interagency Cooperation. Authority: 16 U.S.C. 1531 et seq. Section 7(a)(2): Each Federal agency shall, in consultation with and with the assistance of the Secretary, insure that any action authorized, funded, or carried out by such agency is not likely to jeopardize the continued existence of any endangered species or threatened species or result in the destruction or adverse modification of habitat of such species which is determined to be critical. Biological assessment required for major construction activities. Action area: all areas to be affected directly or indirectly by the Federal action. Jeopardy standard: appreciably reduce the likelihood of both the survival and recovery of a listed species in the wild. Adverse modification standard: appreciably diminish the value of critical habitat for conservation.

records.Name: 54 U.S.C. 306108 — NHPA Section 106 Historic Preservation Review
records.Subject: 54 U.S.C. 306108; 36 CFR Part 800
records.nepa_compliance_requirements__c: Federal agencies must consider effects of their undertakings on historic properties listed or eligible for listing in the National Register of Historic Places and provide the Advisory Council on Historic Preservation a reasonable opportunity to comment. Four sequential steps: (1) initiate process, define area of potential effects (APE); (2) identify historic properties through reasonable/good-faith effort with SHPO/THPO; (3) assess adverse effects; (4) resolve adverse effects via MOA with SHPO/THPO and ACHP. Adverse effect: undertaking diminishes integrity of location, design, setting, materials, workmanship, feeling, or association. Indian tribes and Native Hawaiian organizations with cultural affiliation in APE are mandatory consulting parties.
records.nepa_text_content__c: 36 CFR Part 800 — Protection of Historic Properties. Authority: 54 U.S.C. 306108. Undertaking: a project, activity, or program funded in whole or in part under the direct or indirect jurisdiction of a Federal agency, including those carried out by or on behalf of a Federal agency and those requiring a Federal permit, license, or approval. Area of Potential Effects (APE): the geographic area or areas within which an undertaking may directly or indirectly cause alterations in the character or use of historic properties. Programmatic Agreement used when effects on historic properties cannot be fully determined prior to approval or when multiple undertakings are involved. Failure to complete Section 106 does not automatically stop a federal action but exposes agency to legal challenge under the APA.

records.Name: 16 U.S.C. 1456 — CZMA Section 307 Federal Consistency
records.Subject: 16 U.S.C. 1456; 15 CFR Part 930 Subpart D
records.nepa_compliance_requirements__c: Applicants for federal licenses or permits for activities affecting coastal uses or resources of states with approved CZM programs must certify that the activity is consistent with the state's enforceable CZM policies. The federal agency cannot issue the license or permit if the state objects to the certification (unless overridden on Secretarial appeal). Coverage extends to activities outside the coastal zone with reasonably foreseeable coastal effects. State has 6 months to concur or object after receiving a complete submission; concurrence is presumed after 6 months. Renewals and major amendments with substantially different coastal effects trigger a new review.
records.nepa_text_content__c: 15 CFR Part 930 Subpart D — Consistency for Activities Requiring a Federal License or Permit. Authority: 16 U.S.C. 1456. Applicant consistency certification required under § 930.57. Coastal effects evaluation must accompany the federal permit application and consistency certification. State review period: 6 months from receipt of complete submission (§ 930.62); state concurrence presumed if no response. Objection triggers review; federal agency cannot issue permit over state objection absent Secretarial appeal under Subpart H. States with approved CZM programs: 34 coastal states and territories as of 2025. Note: CZMA Section 307(c)(3)(A) for licenses/permits is distinct from Section 307(c)(1) for federal agency activities and Section 307(c)(2) for OCS plans.

records.Name: 33 U.S.C. 403 — Rivers and Harbors Act Section 10 (Structures in Navigable Waters)
records.Subject: 33 U.S.C. 403; 33 CFR Part 322
records.nepa_compliance_requirements__c: Construction or modification of any structure in or over navigable waters of the United States, or excavation from or fill into navigable waters, requires a permit from the Army Corps of Engineers. Covers wharves, piers, jetties, weirs, breakwaters, bulkheads, revetments, and overhead transmission lines. Applies to all navigable waters defined by tidal influence or current/historical/potential use for interstate or foreign commerce. Nearly always required in conjunction with CWA Section 404 permits for waterway construction projects. USACE processes RHA Section 10 and CWA Section 404 applications jointly using the same application form (ENG 4345).
records.nepa_text_content__c: 33 CFR Part 322 — Permits for Structures or Work in or Affecting Navigable Waters of the United States. Authority: Section 10 of the River and Harbor Act of 1899 (33 U.S.C. 403). Covered activities: creation of any obstruction not affirmatively authorized by Congress; excavation from or fill in navigable waters; construction of any structure in navigable waters. Section 10 permit issued as part of USACE public interest review under 33 CFR 320.4. Navigable waters defined at 33 CFR 329 — includes all waters subject to the ebb and flow of the tide and all waters that are presently used or have been used in the past, or may be susceptible to use to transport interstate or foreign commerce. Note: RHA Section 9 (33 CFR Part 321) covers dams and dikes that completely span a navigable water — a distinct and higher-threshold authorization.

records.Name: 33 U.S.C. 401 — Rivers and Harbors Act Section 9 (Dams Spanning Navigable Waters)
records.Subject: 33 U.S.C. 401; 33 CFR Part 321
records.nepa_compliance_requirements__c: Construction of any dam or dike that completely spans a navigable water of the United States requires congressional approval for interstate waters, state legislative approval for intrastate waters, AND a Corps of Engineers permit. This is one of the highest-threshold federal authorizations in environmental law. Applies to hydropower dams, navigation dams, flood control structures, and tidal barriers. FERC hydropower licenses are issued under FPA Section 4(e) and satisfy the Section 9 authorization requirement for licensed hydropower facilities.
records.nepa_text_content__c: 33 CFR Part 321 — Permits for Dams and Dikes in Navigable Waters of the United States. Authority: 33 U.S.C. 401. Covered: any dam or dike across or in any navigable water of the US that could obstruct interstate or foreign commerce. Threshold: structure must completely span the navigable water. Congressional consent required for interstate waters before Corps permit issued. State legislative approval required for intrastate navigable waters. Note: this Congressional consent requirement is distinct from the Section 10 permit process and applies specifically to impoundment structures. Weirs and partial obstructions are regulated under Section 10 (33 CFR Part 322). For FERC-licensed hydropower, the FERC license provides the required authorization equivalent. Navigation improvements by USACE under its own civil works program are separately authorized.

records.Name: 16 U.S.C. 797 — Federal Power Act Section 4(e) FERC Hydropower License
records.Subject: 16 U.S.C. 797; 18 CFR Part 4
records.nepa_compliance_requirements__c: FERC issues licenses under Federal Power Act Section 4(e) for non-federal hydroelectric projects at government dams and Section 5 for projects on federal lands. Licenses authorize construction, operation, and maintenance of dams, reservoirs, powerhouses, and transmission lines in navigable waters or on federal lands. FERC conducts NEPA review as part of the licensing process. Conditions imposed by resource agencies (USFS, BLM, FWS) under Section 4(e) mandatory conditioning authority are binding on the license. Term: up to 50 years; relicensing required upon expiration. FERC's pre-filing consultation process is credited with reducing median EIS timeline to 1.88 years.
records.nepa_text_content__c: 18 CFR Part 4 — Applications for Licenses, Amendments of License, and Regulatory Approvals. Section 4(e) licenses cover projects at or affecting navigation of navigable waters. Section 4(e) conditions: Secretary of Agriculture (for National Forest System lands) and Secretary of Interior (for other federal lands) have mandatory conditioning authority — their conditions must be included in the FERC license. CWA 401 certification from each state in which project is located required before license issuance. Pre-filing consultation: FERC's pre-filing process under 18 CFR 4.38 requires proponent to consult with resource agencies, tribes, and the public before submitting a license application, substantially front-loading issue identification.

records.Name: 15 U.S.C. 717f — Natural Gas Act Section 7(c) FERC Certificate
records.Subject: 15 U.S.C. 717f; 18 CFR Part 157
records.nepa_compliance_requirements__c: No natural gas company may construct or extend a natural gas pipeline facility subject to FERC jurisdiction without first obtaining a Certificate of Public Convenience and Necessity from FERC under NGA Section 7(c). FERC serves as the lead federal agency for interstate natural gas pipeline NEPA review. Co-permits required from USACE (Section 404 and Section 10), state water quality agencies (401 certification), and resource agencies (ESA, NHPA, CZMA where applicable). FERC's pre-filing process (18 CFR 157.21) requires proponent to initiate resource agency and stakeholder consultation before filing the certificate application. Mountain Valley Pipeline and Atlantic Coast Pipeline 4th Circuit decisions set hostile GHG and ESA precedent.
records.nepa_text_content__c: 18 CFR Part 157 — Applications for Certificates of Public Convenience and Necessity and for Orders Permitting and Approving Abandonment Under Section 7 of the NGA. Certificate of public convenience and necessity required for: (1) construction or extension of facilities for transportation of natural gas; (2) acquisition of existing facilities; (3) extension or enlargement of natural gas company operations. FERC conducts EIS or EA (depending on project size) as part of certificate proceeding. Cooperating agencies include USACE, USFWS, EPA, applicable SHPOs, and federal land management agencies with jurisdiction over affected lands. 4th Circuit Note: Energy projects in VA, WV, NC, SC, MD face 28.6% agency win rate in NEPA litigation — lowest of any sector-circuit cell.

records.Name: 43 U.S.C. 1761 — FLPMA Title V Right-of-Way Grants on BLM/Federal Lands
records.Subject: 43 U.S.C. 1761; 43 CFR Part 2800
records.nepa_compliance_requirements__c: Title V of FLPMA authorizes the Secretary of Interior to grant, issue, and renew rights-of-way over federal lands administered by BLM for pipelines, transmission lines, roads, and other linear infrastructure. BLM conducts NEPA review as part of ROW grant processing. Trigger: any ROW across BLM-administered lands for linear infrastructure projects. Duration: term ROWs for commercial projects; permanent ROWs for federal use. BLM must balance resource protection against reasonable ROW access. Rent calculated based on fair market value. ROW grant conditions may require bonding, environmental monitoring, and reclamation plans.
records.nepa_text_content__c: 43 CFR Part 2800 — Rights-of-Way Under the Federal Land Policy and Management Act. Authority: 43 U.S.C. 1761-1771. Eligible ROW holders: federal, state, or local government entities; citizens of the US; corporations doing business in the US; partnerships; associations; tribes. Application required for all new ROWs; includes project description, maps, plan of development, environmental report. BLM processes ROW application concurrently with NEPA review. For pipelines: ROW grant is the authorizing instrument and must be issued before construction begins. For federal lands managed by USFS, the analog authorization is a Special Use Permit under 36 CFR Part 251.

records.Name: 16 U.S.C. 1604 — NFMA National Forest Management Act Forest Plan
records.Subject: 16 U.S.C. 1604; 36 CFR Part 219
records.nepa_compliance_requirements__c: NFMA requires the Forest Service to develop, maintain, and revise land and resource management plans (Forest Plans) for each National Forest and National Grassland. All site-specific projects and activities must be consistent with the applicable Forest Plan. NEPA review for site-specific projects must demonstrate conformance with the Forest Plan. Forest Plan amendments and revisions require NEPA review (EIS or EA depending on scope). Trigger for project-level review: any project on National Forest System lands including logging, mining, ROW, recreation facilities, and infrastructure.
records.nepa_text_content__c: 36 CFR Part 219 — Planning for Sustainability of National Forest System Resources. Authority: 16 U.S.C. 1604, 1611-1614. Forest Plan provides the framework for project-level decisions — all projects must be consistent with the Forest Plan or a plan amendment must be prepared concurrently. Forest Plan consistency check required before any site-specific NEPA decision. Designated areas (Wilderness, WSRs, roadless areas) impose additional restrictions on project types and intensities. Species viability requirement: plans must include provisions to maintain viable populations of native and desired non-native species. Critical habitat, old-growth, and riparian area standards are enforceable plan components. NFMA Section 14 prohibits harvesting timber from lands not suitable for timber production.

records.Name: 49 U.S.C. 303 — Department of Transportation Act Section 4(f)
records.Subject: 49 U.S.C. 303; 23 CFR Part 774
records.nepa_compliance_requirements__c: Section 4(f) prohibits FHWA, FTA, FRA, and FAA from approving a program or project requiring use of land from a publicly owned public park, recreation area, wildlife/waterfowl refuge, or historic site unless: (1) there is no prudent and feasible alternative, and (2) the project includes all possible planning to minimize harm. Constructive use (noise/visual/access impacts causing substantial impairment) also constitutes a Section 4(f) use. A formal 4(f) evaluation must be completed and approved by the FHWA/FTA Division Administrator. De minimis impact finding available when the use does not adversely affect the activities, features, or attributes qualifying the property for protection.
records.nepa_text_content__c: 23 CFR Part 774 — Parks, Recreation Areas, Wildlife and Waterfowl Refuges, and Historic Sites (Section 4(f)). Authority: 49 U.S.C. 303, 23 U.S.C. 138. Section 4(f) property: land of publicly owned public park, recreation area, wildlife or waterfowl refuge; or land of a historic site of national, state, or local significance. Use: permanent incorporation of 4(f) property into a transportation facility; temporary occupancy that adversely affects activities, features, or attributes qualifying for protection; constructive use (no physical taking but substantially impairs). De minimis finding: if an official with jurisdiction agrees in writing that the project will have no adverse effect on the activities, features, and attributes qualifying the property. FHWA/FTA must consult with the official with jurisdiction before making the finding.

records.Name: 16 U.S.C. 1278 — Wild and Scenic Rivers Act Section 7
records.Subject: 16 U.S.C. 1278; 36 CFR Part 297
records.nepa_compliance_requirements__c: Section 7 of the WSRA prohibits federal agencies from assisting water resources projects that would have a direct and adverse effect on the values for which a river was designated as wild, scenic, or recreational. Applies to designated rivers in the national system and rivers under study for addition. Federal agencies must affirmatively determine that a project will not have a direct and adverse effect before issuing a permit or other authorization. Trigger: any project within or affecting a designated or study river corridor. USFS/NPS/BLM river management plans set specific standards for what constitutes a direct and adverse effect.
records.nepa_text_content__c: 16 U.S.C. 1278(a): No department or agency of the United States shall assist by loan, grant, license, or otherwise in the construction of any water resources project that would have a direct and adverse effect on the values for which such river was established. Section 7(b) applies to rivers in study status — agencies must notify Congress before licensing. Management standards vary by designation class: Wild (most primitive, no impoundment, roads, or motorized use); Scenic (some impoundment and road access permitted outside corridor); Recreational (significant development permitted, accessible by roads). FERC hydropower licensing on or near WSR-designated rivers requires coordination with river-managing agency and Section 7 determination before license issuance.

records.Name: 43 U.S.C. 1712 — FLPMA Resource Management Planning
records.Subject: 43 U.S.C. 1712; 43 CFR Part 1600
records.nepa_compliance_requirements__c: FLPMA Section 202 requires BLM to develop, maintain, and revise land use plans (Resource Management Plans) for all BLM-administered lands. All BLM decisions and authorizations (including ROW grants, APDs, grazing permits, and recreation permits) must conform to the applicable RMP. Project-level NEPA review must demonstrate RMP conformance. Trigger: any project on BLM-administered lands requiring a decision or authorization. RMP amendments and revisions require NEPA review (EIS or EA depending on scope). Multiple-use mandate: BLM must balance livestock grazing, mineral extraction, recreation, wilderness, and conservation.
records.nepa_text_content__c: 43 CFR Part 1600 — Planning for Resource Management. Authority: 43 U.S.C. 1712. Resource Management Plan: the fundamental land use planning document for BLM — establishes allowable uses, management actions, and resource objectives for all resources. All BLM authorizations must be in conformance with the applicable RMP or a concurrent RMP amendment must be processed. Conformance determination required as part of NEPA documentation. RMP amendments classified as significant or non-significant; significant amendments require full NEPA review (usually EIS). The 2022–2023 BLM Public Lands Rule (88 FR 19583) introduced conservation as a co-equal multiple-use objective — subject to ongoing legal challenge.

records.Name: 43 CFR Part 3162 — BLM Oil and Gas Operations (APD)
records.Subject: 43 CFR Part 3162; 43 U.S.C. 1732
records.nepa_compliance_requirements__c: An Application for Permit to Drill (APD) is required before drilling any oil or gas well on BLM-administered lands or on split-estate lands with federal minerals. BLM processes APDs under NEPA with a site-specific EA or CE determination. Trigger: any new well, workover, or enhanced recovery operation on federal mineral estate. Surface use plan required with APD; reclamation bond required before drilling begins. EPAct 2005 Section 390 authorized CEs for 5 categories of oil and gas actions (routine well drilling, well recompletion, pipeline rights-of-way, water injection wells, and similar low-disturbance actions) subject to the 5-acre/150-acre thresholds.
records.nepa_text_content__c: 43 CFR 3162.3-1: Application for Permit to Drill (APD). Required before commencement of any drilling operation on federal or Indian lands. Includes: well plat, proposed drilling program, surface use plan of operations, bond. BLM has 10 business days to determine completeness; processing target: 30 days for CE, 6 months for EA, 2+ years for EIS. EPAct 2005 Section 390 CE: available for new oil/gas wells < 5 acres surface disturbance within a lease area with < 150 acres total surface disturbance; applicable only on previously disturbed ground with an existing road providing access. Not available in extraordinary circumstances (T&E habitat, Wild & Scenic River corridor, NHPA Section 106 not concluded).

records.Name: 30 U.S.C. 1257 — SMCRA Surface Mining Control and Reclamation Act Permit
records.Subject: 30 U.S.C. 1257; 30 CFR Parts 700–777
records.nepa_compliance_requirements__c: A permit from the Office of Surface Mining Reclamation and Enforcement (OSMRE) or an approved state regulatory authority is required before commencing surface coal mining and reclamation operations. Permit application requires environmental resource information, mining/reclamation plan, performance bond, and public notice. Federal OSMRE conducts NEPA review for permits on federal coal leases; state-regulated permits on private lands typically do not require federal NEPA review. Trigger: any surface disturbance associated with surface coal mining (including overburden removal, coal extraction, transportation, and reclamation).
records.nepa_text_content__c: 30 CFR Part 777 — General Content Requirements for Permit Applications. Authority: 30 U.S.C. 1257. Application requires: mining/reclamation plan; ground and surface water baseline; vegetation and wildlife baseline; prime farmland assessment; historical and archaeological resource assessment; geological data; hydrological impact analysis. Performance bond: minimum $10,000/acre or sufficient to cover reclamation cost. Approximate original contour (AOC) restoration required. Alluvial valley floors: mining in or near alluvial valley floors of arid and semi-arid regions prohibited unless pre-existing operations. Section 522 of SMCRA: citizens may petition to designate areas as unsuitable for mining.

records.Name: 49 U.S.C. 10901 — Surface Transportation Board Certificate of Public Convenience and Necessity
records.Subject: 49 U.S.C. 10901; 49 CFR Part 1150
records.nepa_compliance_requirements__c: A Surface Transportation Board (STB) Certificate of Public Convenience and Necessity is required before constructing and operating a new rail line or acquiring an existing rail line. STB serves as the lead agency for NEPA review of new railroad construction. Co-permits include CWA Section 404, Section 4(f) for transportation projects, ESA Section 7, and NHPA Section 106. STB may exempt minor construction from full certificate requirements. STB preempts state and local permitting for rail line construction under 49 U.S.C. 10501(b).
records.nepa_text_content__c: 49 CFR Part 1150 — Exemptions for Certain Acquisitions, Construction, and Operations. Authority: 49 U.S.C. 10901, 10502. Certificate required: before any person may construct or acquire any railroad line or operate as a rail carrier. STB conducts environmental review under NEPA; typically an EA for smaller projects and EIS for major new lines. Exemption process: STB may grant exemptions from certificate requirements for small line construction, certain track changes, and terminal operations. Preemption: 49 U.S.C. 10501(b) preempts state and local regulation of rail transportation; STB environmental review replaces state environmental permitting. EIS projects: major new interstate rail lines through previously undisturbed corridors typically require full EIS.

records.Name: 42 U.S.C. 7661 — Clean Air Act Title V Operating Permit Program
records.Subject: 42 U.S.C. 7661 et seq.; 40 CFR Part 70
records.nepa_compliance_requirements__c: Title V of the Clean Air Act requires major stationary sources of air pollution (typically emitting 100+ tons per year of regulated pollutants) to obtain an operating permit from EPA or an approved state program. Operating permits consolidate all applicable CAA requirements into a single permit. Trigger: major source thresholds vary by pollutant and attainment status — 100 tpy in attainment areas, 10-50 tpy in nonattainment areas depending on severity classification. Sources include large power plants, industrial facilities, refineries, and smelters. New sources in PSD areas must also obtain a Prevention of Significant Deterioration permit under Sections 165–167.
records.nepa_text_content__c: 40 CFR Part 70 — State Operating Permit Programs (Title V). Authority: 42 U.S.C. 7661-7661f. Major source: >= 100 tpy of any regulated air pollutant in attainment areas; lower thresholds (10-50 tpy) in nonattainment areas. Application completeness determination: 60 days. Permit issuance: 18 months maximum from complete application. Public comment: 30-day minimum comment period on draft permit. EPA review: 45 days to object to draft permit. Affected state review required for permits affecting downwind states. Compliance certification: annual reporting required. NSR/PSD separate from Title V — NSR required before construction of new major sources or major modifications.

records.Name: 42 U.S.C. 6925 — RCRA Subtitle C Hazardous Waste Treatment Storage and Disposal Permit
records.Subject: 42 U.S.C. 6925; 40 CFR Part 270
records.nepa_compliance_requirements__c: A RCRA Subtitle C permit is required for any facility that treats, stores, or disposes of hazardous waste as defined under RCRA Section 3001. Trigger: treatment, storage (> 90 days), or disposal of RCRA-listed or characteristic hazardous waste. Permit process: permit application, Part A (facility info) and Part B (detailed engineering/environmental info); public notice and comment; corrective action requirements for releases. NEPA typically not required for RCRA permits themselves (RCRA has its own environmental review process), but federal NEPA review required where a federal agency is the permit applicant or co-lead.
records.nepa_text_content__c: 40 CFR Part 270 — EPA Administered Permit Programs: The Hazardous Waste Permit Program. Authority: 42 U.S.C. 6925, 6926. Hazardous waste facility permit required for: treatment (includes incineration, neutralization, stabilization); storage (tanks, containers, piles, surface impoundments, waste piles, drip pads); disposal (landfills, land treatment, underground injection). Part A: basic facility identification, design capacity, processes. Part B: groundwater monitoring, closure/post-closure plan, corrective action program, waste analysis plan, emergency response. Interim status: facilities in operation on Nov 19, 1980 may operate under interim status until permit issued. Post-closure care: 30-year post-closure monitoring minimum for land disposal units.

records.Name: 42 U.S.C. 300h — SDWA Underground Injection Control Program
records.Subject: 42 U.S.C. 300h; 40 CFR Parts 144–148
records.nepa_compliance_requirements__c: The Safe Drinking Water Act Underground Injection Control (UIC) program requires permits for all injection wells that place fluids into the ground. Five UIC well classes: Class I (industrial/municipal waste disposal); Class II (oil and gas related — produced water, enhanced recovery, hydrocarbon storage); Class III (mineral extraction); Class IV (hazardous waste disposal — banned); Class V (all others including geothermal wells, drain fields). Trigger: any subsurface emplacement of fluids through a bored, drilled, or driven well. Class II wells most common for energy projects (produced water disposal, CO2 sequestration, geothermal).
records.nepa_text_content__c: 40 CFR Part 144 — Underground Injection Control Program. Authority: 42 U.S.C. 300h. Permit required before construction of any injection well. Prohibition on injection that endangers underground sources of drinking water (USDWs). Class II requirements: mechanical integrity testing (MIT) required annually; injection zone must be isolated from USDWs by at least one confining zone; annulus pressure monitored continuously. Class VI (geologic sequestration of CO2): highest requirements including 3D reservoir model, computational modeling, 50-year monitoring plan. Primacy: 32 states and territories have EPA-approved UIC programs; remaining states/territories regulated directly by EPA.

records.Name: 43 U.S.C. 1331 — Outer Continental Shelf Lands Act (OCSLA) Lease and Plan Approval
records.Subject: 43 U.S.C. 1331 et seq.; 30 CFR Part 550
records.nepa_compliance_requirements__c: BOEM manages oil and gas, wind, and other energy leasing on the Outer Continental Shelf under OCSLA. Offshore energy development requires lease issuance, approval of an Exploration Plan (EP) and Development and Production Plan (DPP) or Development Operations Coordination Document (DOCD), and well permits. BOEM conducts NEPA review at the programmatic level (5-year leasing program) and project level (EP/DPP). CZMA federal consistency review required for all OCS plans. ESA and MMPA consultations required when listed species may be affected. BOEM achieves 100% litigation win rate and 2.24-year median EIS timeline.
records.nepa_text_content__c: 30 CFR Part 550 — Oil and Gas and Sulphur Operations in the Outer Continental Shelf. Authority: 43 U.S.C. 1331. Leasing: competitive lease sales conducted by BOEM following 5-year National OCS Program (NEPA EIS required). Exploration Plan approval: required before drilling; BOEM has 30 days to review. DPP/DOCD approval: required before development/production; BOEM has 60 days to review. Well permits: separate applications to BSEE (Bureau of Safety and Environmental Enforcement) for each well. CZMA consistency: OCS plans require coastal state consultation under 15 CFR Part 930 Subpart E. ESA: BOEM conducts programmatic consultation; project-specific effects addressed in EP/DPP review.

records.Name: 16 U.S.C. 703 — Migratory Bird Treaty Act
records.Subject: 16 U.S.C. 703 et seq.; 50 CFR Part 21
records.nepa_compliance_requirements__c: MBTA prohibits the taking, killing, or possession of migratory birds protected under US treaties with Canada, Mexico, Japan, and Russia without a federal permit. Take includes incidental take from lawful activities (construction, operation, maintenance) where birds or their nests/eggs are harmed. USFWS encourages Programmatic Agreements and conservation plans to minimize incidental take. Specific permit (Special Purpose Permit) required for intentional take; incidental take guidance published for energy projects (especially wind and transmission). Trigger: any activity that may result in injury or death to migratory birds listed under 50 CFR Part 10.
records.nepa_text_content__c: 50 CFR Part 21 — Migratory Bird Permits. Authority: 16 U.S.C. 703-712. Protected species: approximately 1,093 species listed in 50 CFR 10.13. No take permit: MBTA has traditionally been interpreted as strict liability for incidental take (under Obama administration guidance); 2021 Biden administration M-Opinion returned to strict liability interpretation. Take avoidance: nest surveys required before ground-disturbing activities during nesting season (species and location specific). Wind energy: USFWS Land-Based Wind Energy Guidelines recommend 3-year pre-construction monitoring. Solar energy: high-intensity glare and thermal effects on birds regulated through MBTA incidental take guidance. Programmatic take permits: Eagle Conservation Plans (for golden and bald eagles under Bald and Golden Eagle Protection Act, 16 U.S.C. 668).
